title: After a 10-year search, the gravitational constant is more mysterious than ever
html_body:
What matters in science |
View this email in your browser Wednesday 22 April 2026

Hello Nature readers,
Today we learn that a 10-year investigation of a universal constant has led to an even bigger mystery. Plus, we gain a deeper appreciation for the impact of X and Y chromosomes and ask why malaria is rising despite the advent of vaccines.

If you enjoy this newsletter, please consider recommending it to a friend or colleague. Click here to forward it by e-mail. Thank you!
Physicists at the US National Institute of Standards and Technology decided to meticulously measure Big G after a 2014 crisis meeting brought rival experimenters together to try to hash out a way forward. (R. Eskalis/NIST)

‘Big G’ just keeps getting weirder

The results of a decade-long experiment have brought scientists no closer to pinning down the value of Big G, the constant that describes the gravitational pull between objects. The experiment aimed to replicate research published in 2013, but resulted in a number that disagreed with those results and with the value that’s considered the best current estimate of G. The work is “soul draining”, says physicist Stephan Schlamminger, co-author of the latest study. But he is driven by the challenge. “It must be possible for humans to measure this number.”

Nature | 5 min read

US speeds research into psychedelics

US President Donald Trump signed an executive order to streamline research into psychedelic compounds and to make it easier for people with certain illnesses to access these drugs. It calls out by name the potent and long-lasting psychedelic ibogaine, which is something of a scientific mystery, in part because it is one of the most tightly controlled drugs in the United States. The move has been welcomed by researchers who see potential for drugs, including psilocybin and MDMA, in treating conditions such as addiction, depression and post-traumatic stress disorder. But scientists are also concerned about possible side effects, and the impact on health services if the drugs are approved.

Nature | 7 min read

Infographic of the week
More than two-thirds of the public believe at least one false or unproven health claim, according to a survey across 16 countries. People who believed three or more of the claims were as likely to have attended university and more likely to consume health news than were those who believed fewer of them, suggesting that a proliferation of conflicting information is a bigger problem than simply being ill-informed. (Nature | 7 min read)

Reference: 2026 Edelman Trust Barometer (Source: 2026 Edelman Trust Barometer report)

Features & opinion

Sex chromosomes are full of surprises

A rising wave of evidence is showing that genes on the sex chromosomes — usually XX in women and XY in men — can have a substantial impact on health and disease. Genes that escape the ‘silencing’ process on one X chromosome seem to be a key reason why around 80% of people with autoimmune disease are women, for example. The escapees could also have a protective effect: they could help to explain why autism is more common in males than in females, for example. Meanwhile, X’s diminutive partner, Y, might be contributing to more than just sex determination.

Nature | 11 min read

Can screening stop the rise in young cancer?

More young people are getting cancer — so countries including Australia, Canada, South Korea and the United States are lowering the screening age for breast, colorectal and other cancers. But screening is expensive, and isn’t without risks: it can turn up false positives and lead to unnecessary treatments. “It’s a teachable moment for the public about screening,” says physician and medical historian Barron Lerner. Meanwhile, in low- and middle-income countries, which account for the majority of new cancer cases and deaths worldwide, cancer screening is often extremely limited, even for the most at-risk patients.

Nature Index | 11 min read
 Flora & Felicity (Bec)

A Venice saved is a Venice changed

Three researchers who were on a team that analysed how to protect Venice from sea-level rise say that “radical measures may be necessary” to save the iconic canal city. If rising waters — and the fact that the land itself is sinking under its own weight — overwhelm the current system of mobile barriers, the city might have to be ringed in dykes or even relocated altogether. Measures to raise the city, such as by injecting sea water into the rocks deep underground, could keep the current barriers effective at up to a metre of sea-level rise, but that looks unlikely to be sufficient.

The Conversation | 6 min read
Reference: Scientific Reports paper

Quote of the day

“Some diseases are genuinely difficult, if not impossible, to eliminate. Malaria is not one of them.”

Despite the advent of vaccines, malaria deaths are rising, not falling, notes a Nature editorial. It echoes this year’s World Malaria Day slogan: “Now we can. Now we must.” (7 min read)

I’m enjoying Earth day by getting an astronauts-eye-view of our home from the International Space Station thanks to Sen, Sen, the first continuous 4K video livestream from space. Since January of last year, Sen has been broadcasting about 20 hours a day of our planet’s infinite beauty. Here’s a particularly stunning view of the home of Nature, the United Kingdom, captured earlier today.

While I try to spot Briefing towers, why not send me your feedback on this newsletter? Your e-mails are always welcome at briefing@nature.com.

Thanks for reading,
Flora Graham, senior editor, Nature Briefing
With contributions by Jacob Smith

items.title: ‘Big G ’ just keeps getting weirder
items.snippet: The results of a decade-long experiment have brought scientists no closer to pinning down the value of Big G , the constant that describes the gravitational pull between objects. The experiment aimed to replicate research published in 2013, but resulted in a number that disagreed with those results and with the value that’s considered the best current estimate of G . The work is “soul draining”, says physicist Stephan Schlamminger, co-author of the latest study. But he is driven by the challenge. “It must be possible for humans to measure this number.”

items.title: US speeds research into psychedelics
items.snippet: US President Donald Trump signed an executive order to streamline research into psychedelic compounds and to make it easier for people with certain illnesses to access these drugs. It calls out by name the potent and long-lasting psychedelic ibogaine, which is something of a scientific mystery, in part because it is one of the most tightly controlled drugs in the United States. The move has been welcomed by researchers who see potential for drugs, including psilocybin and MDMA, in treating conditions such as addiction, depression and post-traumatic stress disorder. But scientists are also concerned about possible side effects, and the impact on health services if the drugs are approved.

items.snippet: A rising wave of evidence is showing that genes on the sex chromosomes — usually XX in women and XY in men — can have a substantial impact on health and disease . Genes that escape the ‘silencing’ process on one X chromosome seem to be a key reason why around 80% of people with autoimmune disease are women, for example. The escapees could also have a protective effect: they could help to explain why autism is more common in males than in females, for example. Meanwhile, X’s diminutive partner, Y, might be contributing to more than just sex determination.

items.snippet: More young people are getting cancer — so countries including Australia, Canada, South Korea and the United States are lowering the screening age for breast, colorectal and other cancers. But screening is expensive, and isn’t without risks: it can turn up false positives and lead to unnecessary treatments . “It’s a teachable moment for the public about screening,” says physician and medical historian Barron Lerner. items.snippet: Three researchers who were on a team that analysed how to protect Venice from sea-level rise say that “radical measures may be necessary” to save the iconic canal city. If rising waters — and the fact that the land itself is sinking under its own weight — overwhelm the current system of mobile barriers, the city might have to be ringed in dykes or even relocated altogether . summary.overview: 10년간의 연구에도 불구하고 중력 상수 'Big G'의 정확한 값이 여전히 불확실해지며, 이에 대한 과학적 의문이 깊어졌습니다. 또한 심리활성 물질 연구 가속화, 성 염색체와 건강의 관계, 젊은 세대 암 예방 전략, 베니스 보호를 위한 혁신적 접근 등 다양한 주제가 다뤄졌습니다.
covered_item_titles: Sex chromosomes are full of surprises
Can screening stop the rise in young cancer?
A Venice saved is a Venice changed

article_briefings.title: ‘Big G’ just keeps getting weirder
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 10년간의 실험 결과로 중력 상수 'Big G'의 값을 정확히 파악하지 못해 과학자들이 더 큰 의문을 품게 되었습니다. 2013년 연구를 재현하려는 실험에도 불구하고 값이 여전히 불확실합니다.

article_briefings.title: US speeds research into psychedelics
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 트럼프 대통령이 심리활성 물질 연구를 가속화하고 특정 질병 환자의 약물 접근을 용이하게 하는 행정명령을 서명했습니다. 특히 강력한 ibogaine이라는 물질에 대한 연구가 주목받고 있습니다.

article_briefings.title: Sex chromosomes are full of surprises
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 성 염색체에 위치한 유전자가 건강과 질병에 큰 영향을 미치는 것으로 나타났습니다. 한 X 염색체에서 '침묵화'가 피하는 유전자들이 주요 원인으로 작용하고 있습니다.

article_briefings.title: Can screening stop the rise in young cancer?
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 젊은 세대 암 발생 증가에 따라 유방암, 대장암 등 검진 연령이 낮아지고 있지만, 검진 비용과 과도한 진단 위험 등 문제가 지속적으로 제기되고 있습니다.

article_briefings.title: A Venice saved is a Venice changed
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 해수면 상승과 지하 침하로 인한 베니스 보호를 위해 '혁신적인 조치가 필요하다'는 주장이 나왔습니다. 연구자들은 극단적인 방안을 검토해야 한다고 강조했습니다.

raw_metadata.subject: After a 10-year search, the gravitational constant is more mysterious than ever
